title: How Seed Oils Are The Main Driver Of Disease with Chris Knobbeith

chunks.title: Introduction to Seed Oils
chunks.summary: The podcast opens with a discussion about the dangers of seed oils, which are suggested to be significant contributors to chronic illnesses, potentially more harmful than sugar. The speaker emphasizes the importance of eliminating these oils from diets for better health.
chunks.transcript: on this week's podcast I had the pleasure of talking to my friend ophthalmologist Dr Chris Kenobi and we talked about seed oils it's a topic I've spoken about many times in the past but I think it cannot be talked about enough because by my estimation and Chris is these are probably the single greatest driver of chronic illness in humans today yes greater than sugar as you will see in this podcast and we talk about some very interesting observational research to corroborate that assertion and so if you do one thing getting rid of seed oils in your diet or significantly limiting them I believe will make a massive impact on your overall health so that's why this conversation with Chris is critically important that's why I think talking about seed oils is important these are corn canola sunflower safflower so enjoy this conversation with Chris Kenobi you will learn a ton we dig into a lot of the science but it's not overly scientific as some of the previous podcasts on cdos have been regardless the take home here is get rid of seed oils in your diet completely corn canola sunflower soybean safflower all that kind of garbage and

chunks.title: Dr. Chris Kenobi's Background
chunks.summary: Dr. Chris Kenobi, an ophthalmologist turned nutrition researcher, shares his journey of leaving practice to investigate the links between processed foods, vegetable oils, and age-related macular degeneration (AMD), which is a leading cause of blindness.
chunks.transcript: thank you Paul appreciate you having me on it's a pleasure Dr Chris Kenobi my mistake doctor welcome doctor so I thought anybody call me Dr Paul a couple of podcasts together but for people that aren't familiar with your background um just can you introduce yourself in your background and then well let's just jump into some Ophthalmology stuff and from the start okay sure so I'm a physician ophthalmologist um had a full career in Ophthalmology and um I am now a nutrition researcher as of the past about eight years I left practice in 2015 to pursue um the hypothesis that processed foods and vegetable oils or the drivers of age-related macular degeneration AMD which is the leading cause of irreversible vision loss and blindness in people over the age of 50 worldwide and so I went down that path uh Paul um you know published a paper went public with that in 2016 and 2017 started a foundation and then by 2018 2019 I just was so convinced that I was seeing so much evidence that the vegetable oils are the primary drivers of obesity overweight and virtually all chronic disease and I just didn't feel it was being covered very well so I so I began to investigate that really in 2018 and went public with that in 2019 that's kind of where I've been ever since and just published a book a new book it's called Uh The ancestral diet Revolution just came out last week um there we go there's a picture of it Paul's holding up and um so that so that's basically my story in a nutshell

chunks.title: Understanding Macular Degeneration
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on age-related macular degeneration (AMD), exploring its causes and the link to diet, particularly the impact of processed foods and vegetable oils. Dr. Kenobi elaborates on his research findings about AMD's historical rarity prior to the introduction of processed foods.
chunks.transcript: macular degeneration for people my father just had surgery on his uh on his uh I guess his lenses for a cataracts which is not the same as macular degeneration but when people's eyes go it significantly affects our quality of life so you mentioned something you mentioned that age-related macular degeneration AMD is the leading cause of blindness or reversible blindness in adults over the age of 50. so it's a big deal in the world right right and you know just so people are aware um you know many people really believe that that I my path through all this was that I I went down the path of trying to figure out what the cause of macular degeneration was and then went Broad and applied that to chronic disease and that might be a a more uh romantic story if it were but but that's really not what happened you know I um in 2013 when I read Western prices research I understood that it was mostly you know refined flowers refined sugars vegetable oils included vegetable fats he called them as part of the processed foods driving all the chronic disease so I was convinced by 2013 that that processed foods and sugars and vegetable oils were a big you know they were the problem with chronic disease and so then I applied that that principle to look at macular

chunks.title: Impact of Vegetable Oils on Health
chunks.summary: Dr. Kenobi presents evidence correlating vegetable oil consumption with various chronic diseases, including obesity and macular degeneration. He discusses the troubling increase in AMD cases correlating with the rise in vegetable oil usage.
chunks.transcript: November 2017 published in medical hypotheses and in every single country you know the Big Driver it's clear is vegetable oils and in one of those countries one of the things that bothered me about the research initially was the fact that in one of the Pacific island nations kiribas the sugar consumption was really high or you know at least moderately High by the world standards and but their vegetable oil consumption was extremely low practically zero and they had virtually no macro degeneration and so this bothered me at first because I thought you know sugar has to be driving this and and it was one of those moments I thought I don't want to publish This research this this that that piece of I'm like I want to cherry pick this data and we'll present 24 of the Nations and Lead Out the one and then the more I thought about it I kept thinking no wait a minute you know the problem is is maybe maybe sugars are oak you know maybe they're not maybe it's the vegetable oils and eventually and I just kept investigating and we published all the data and I published a book on this and I started a non-profit foundation and and then as I said then I began just looking at this at more and more and more detail in

chunks.title: Mechanisms of Concern
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the mechanisms by which vegetable oils may contribute to chronic diseases, including issues with energetic metabolism and inflammatory processes. The role of cardiolipin in cellular health and how linoleic acid impacts it are explained.

chunks.title: Carboxyethyl Pyrol and Health Implications
chunks.summary: The discussion touches on carboxyethyl pyrol, a breakdown product of linoleic acid, and its potential role in causing autoimmunity and damage to retinal cells, linking it to macular degeneration.
chunks.transcript: heard about this before reading your book and that is very interesting in the retinal pigmented epithelium okay the medical community failed me but my life has now turned around check out this review on whole package from heart and soil supplements Derek says for three years I've struggled with depression anxiety panic attacks night sweats lack of energy and the inability to gain weight I'm a 33 year old male with severely low testosterone I started this supplement less than a week ago and my life has already turned around I have energy and most importantly mental Clarity I'm free of depression and

chunks.title: Linoleic Acid Consumption Trends
chunks.summary: The transcript examines historical trends of linoleic acid consumption and its significant rise in the American diet since the introduction of seed oils. It highlights the consequences of this increased consumption for public health.
chunks.transcript: diet through history this is I think is a critically important because we know that as you and I have talked about and many others have talked about that for example coronary heart disease was virtually unknown in the 19th century Alzheimer's disease was unknown macular degeneration was extraordinarily rare um you know dementia was was unknown unless it came from syphilis um you know obesity was 1.2 percent in man age 18 to 80. so what were we not consuming and the answer is is that well we had extra ordinarily low levels of omega-6 linoleic acid because we didn't have any seed oils in the diet until right after the American Civil War beginning about 1866 we got cotton seed oil that was the first seed oil that Americans had ever seen and they weren't really ready to accept it but if we but what we did was let's get back you know staying with the omega-6 theme is that we modeled the diet of Americans in 1865 and you know you know you could do this too this is not that hard to do when you when you have things to help you like chronometer and you know about the out the amount of La in grass-fed meats and Mill you know milk and purely ancestrally raised animals and all that so we looked at this 1865 Americans and they were consuming about 2.2 to 2.6 grams of La per day now in the book um I used the figure 2.2 grams which is about one percent but it might be 1.1 percent we're splitting hairs here okay in terms of the omega-6 linoleic acid consumption in 1865. all right at that point we had no vegetable oils we were phenomenally healthy 1909 we know it's the next data available comes from um Tonya blasball and we know that by then we had a little bit of cotton seed oil and we had soybean oil we had about nine grams of vegetable oils per day and our total La consumption had doubled there's the graph so we were at 2.4 grams of omega-6 linoleic acid in 1909 that's 2.23 percent of consumption right of total calories and if you go clear in 1999 you can see

chunks.title: Japan's Nutritional Transition
chunks.summary: Dr. Kenobi shares observations from Japan's nutrition transition, noting the correlation between the rise in vegetable oil consumption and increased rates of obesity and chronic diseases, all while sugar consumption decreased.

chunks.title: Comparative Analysis with China
chunks.summary: The podcast continues by analyzing China’s dietary changes over the years, emphasizing the low sugar intake and the rising consumption of vegetable oils, which follows similar trends to those seen in Japan regarding chronic disease rates.

chunks.title: Final Thoughts and Resources
chunks.summary: Dr. Kenobi discusses his book, the foundations he started, and encourages listeners to educate themselves on the harmful effects of seed oils on health. Resources and contact information for further engagement are provided.
chunks.transcript: and typically it Paul all right well thank you man it's good to see you again and I hope that I hope that uh I hope that eventually a lot of people hear about this and turn the tide what's what's encouraging for me is that when I'm out in the in the world these days traveling um some people see me and they say hey you're the meat guy but other people see me and they go seed oils are and so it's making an impact you know I was at arawan in Los Angeles I'm doing a smoothie with them next month in July we're launching the Smoothie July 7th at arawan in Los Angeles it's a carnivore it's like it's not a carnivore but it's like an animal-based raw smoothie with raw kefir and a bunch of fruit and honey it's really gonna be good and I was in the parking lot of arawan talking to a friend and in the span of 15 minutes at least six people walked by and said hey what's up man I love your work and I thought wow that's really cool but multiple people drove by in their cars and said like seed oils get rid of the seed oils seed oils and I thought wow it's the messages getting out people are getting it so that's pretty cool so thanks for the work you do can change the whole world and that's what you know that's what we're doing so we're doing this together right so I appreciate it Paul you're doing such fabulous work all the time sorry my hat's off to you all the time thank you Chris good to see you and until next
